perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Agent pursuant to this Agreement shall terminate upon termination of the Offering, but in no event later than 45 days after the completion of the Subscription Offering, unless extended (the "End Date"). All fees or expenses due to the Agent but unpaid will be payable to the Agent in next day funds at the earlier of the Closing Date (as hereinafter defined) or the End Date.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Agent pursuant to this Agreement shall terminate upon termination of the Offering, but in no event later than 10 days after the completion of the Subscription Offering, unless extended (the "End Date"). All fees or expenses due to the Agent but unpaid will be payable to the Agent in next day funds at the earlier of the Closing Date (as hereinafter defined) or the End Date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination period to 10 days after the offering may not allow sufficient time for reconciliation and payment of all fees, potentially violating regulations related to timely payment for services rendered.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 29 CFR § 516.5 - Records to be preserved for 3 years.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation requires the keeping of records, including those of compensation, for at least 3 years. A 10-day deadline may hinder the Agent's ability to finalize compensation calculations, resolve disputes, and preserve all relevant compensation records in accordance with federal law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 29 CFR § 516.5 - Records to be preserved 3 years. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 29 CFR § 516.5 - Records to be preserved 3 years. CFR prev | next § 516.5 Records to be preserved 3 years. Each employer shall preserve for at least 3 years: (a) Payroll records. From the last date of entry, all payroll or other records containing the employee information and data required under any of the applicable sections of this part, and (b) Certificates, agreements, plans, notices, etc. From their last effective date, all written: (1) Collective bargaining agreements relied upon for the exclusion of certain costs under section 3(m) of the Act, (2) Collective bargaining agreements, under section 7(b)(1) or 7(b)(2) of the Act, and any amendments or additions thereto, (3) Plans, trusts, employment contracts, and collective bargaining agreements under section 7(e) of the Act, (4) Individual contracts or collective bargaining agreements under section 7(f) of the Act. Where such contracts or agreements are not in writing, a written memorandum summarizing the terms of each such contract or agreement, (5) Written agreements or memoranda summarizing the terms of oral agreements or understandings under section 7(g) or 7(j) of the Act, and (6) Certificates and notices listed or named in any applicable section of this part. (c) Sales and purchase records. A record of (1) total dollar volume of sales or business, and (2) total volume of goods purchased or received during such periods (weekly, monthly, quarterly, etc.), in such form as the employer maintains records in the ordinary course of business. perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the termination period from 45 to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that this shorter period could hinder the agent's ability to finalize compensation calculations and preserve records as required by federal law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 29 CFR § 516.5, mandates that employers preserve payroll records for at least 3 years. A 10-day period does not allow sufficient time to ensure records are accurate and preserved for the legally required duration, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Holding Company will not sell or issue, contract to sell or otherwise dispose of, for a period of 90 days after the Closing Date, without the Agent's prior written consent, any of its shares of common stock, other than the Shares or other than in connection with any plan or arrangement described in the Prospectus.
perturbation.changed_text: The Holding Company will not sell or issue, contract to sell or otherwise dispose of, for a period of 30 days after the Closing Date, without the Agent's prior written consent, any of its shares of common stock, other than the Shares or other than in connection with any plan or arrangement described in the Prospectus.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the lock-up period to 30 days could potentially violate regulations concerning insider trading or market manipulation. It may also conflict with standard underwriting practices and agreements, diminishing market confidence in the stock's stability post-offering.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation D's integration rules (17 CFR § 230.502(a)) address the timing and integration of multiple offerings. Reducing the lock-up period to 30 days may raise concerns that subsequent offerings could be improperly integrated with the initial offering, potentially jeopardizing the initial offering's compliance with Regulation D and other securities laws, which often implicitly or explicitly suggest longer safe harbor periods.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.502 - General conditions to be met. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.502 - General conditions to be met. CFR prev | next § 230.502 General conditions to be met. The following conditions shall be applicable to offers and sales made under Regulation D ( § 230.500 et seq. of this chapter): (a) Integration. To determine whether offers and sales should be integrated, see § 230.152 . (b) Information requirements —(1) When information must be furnished. If the issuer sells securities under § 230.506(b) to any purchaser that is not an accredited investor , the issuer shall furnish the information specified in paragraph (b)(2) of this section to such purchaser a reasonable time prior to sale. The issuer is not required to furnish the specified information to purchasers when it sells securities under § 230.504, or to any accredited investor . Note: When an issuer provides information to investors pursuant to paragraph (b)(1), it should consider providing such information to accredited investors as well, in view of the anti-fraud provisions of the federal securities laws. (2) Type of information to be furnished. (i) If the issuer is not subject to the reporting requirements of section 13 or 15(d) of the Exchange Act , at a reasonable time prior to the sale of securities the issuer shall furnish to the purchaser, to the extent material to an understanding of the issuer , its business and the securities being offered: (A) Non-financial statement information. If the issuer is eligible to use Regulation A ( § 230.251-263 ), the same kind of information as would be required in Part II of Form 1-A ( § 239.90 of this chapter). If the issuer is not eligible to use Regulation A, the same kind of information as required in Part I of a registration statement filed under the Securities Act on the form that the issuer would be entitled to use. (B) Financial statement infor
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the lock-up period from 90 to 30 days. The Law Explanation suggests this could lead to improper integration of subsequent offerings, potentially jeopardizing compliance with Regulation D. The Perturbation Explanation states this reduction might violate insider trading regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions Regulation D (§ 230.500 et seq. of this chapter) and its general conditions, including integration considerations under § 230.152. While the snippet doesn't explicitly state a specific lock-up period, the concern that a shorter period could undermine the initial offering's compliance with Regulation D constitutes a legal contradiction. The change creates a time-sensitive inconsistency between standard underwriting practices and legal compliance by reducing the period and potentially running afoul of Regulation D integration rules.

perturbation.original_text: During the period during which the Shares are registered under the 1934 Act or for three years from the date hereof, whichever period is greater, the Holding Company will furnish to its shareholders as soon as practicable after the end of each fiscal year an annual report of the Holding Company (including a consolidated balance sheet and statements of consolidated income, shareholders' equity and cash flows of the Holding Company and its subsidiaries as at the end of and for such year, certified by independent registered public accountants in accordance with Regulation S-X under the 1933 Act and the 1934 Act) and make available as soon as practicable after the end of each of the first three quarters of each fiscal year (beginning with the first fiscal quarter ending after the effective time of the Registration Statement) financial information of the Holding Company and its subsidiaries for such quarter in reasonable detail.
perturbation.changed_text: During the period during which the Shares are registered under the 1934 Act or for one year from the date hereof, whichever period is greater, the Holding Company will furnish to its shareholders as soon as practicable after the end of each fiscal year an annual report of the Holding Company (including a consolidated balance sheet and statements of consolidated income, shareholders' equity and cash flows of the Holding Company and its subsidiaries as at the end of and for such year, certified by independent registered public accountants in accordance with Regulation S-X under the 1933 Act and the 1934 Act) and make available as soon as practicable after the end of each of the first three quarters of each fiscal year (beginning with the first fiscal quarter ending after the effective time of the Registration Statement) financial information of the Holding Company and its subsidiaries for such quarter in reasonable detail.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the reporting period to one year contradicts the SEC's requirements for ongoing disclosure by publicly traded companies. This could violate regulations regarding periodic reporting and transparency.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation mandates that every issuer of a security registered pursuant to Section 12 of the Act (15 U.S.C. 78l) shall file an annual report on the appropriate form authorized or prescribed therefor for each fiscal year after the last full fiscal year for which financial statements were filed in its registration statement. Reducing the report period to one year would be in direct conflict with this regulation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13a-1 - Requirements of annual reports. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13a-1 - Requirements of annual reports. CFR § 240.13a-1 Requirements of annual reports. Every issuer having securities registered pursuant to section 12 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 l ) shall file an annual report on the appropriate form authorized or prescribed therefor for each fiscal year after the last full fiscal year for which financial statements were filed in its registration statement . Annual reports shall be filed within the period specified in the appropriate form. [ 62 FR 39767 , July 24, 1997] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the reporting period from "three years" to "one year." The scraped law snippet 2 (17 CFR § 240.13a-1) states that "Every issuer having securities registered pursuant to section 12 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 l ) shall file an annual report... for each fiscal year after the last full fiscal year for which financial statements were filed in its registration statement." By reducing the reporting period, the changed text contradicts the law requiring annual reports for each fiscal year, making it non-compliant and therefore a legal contradiction.
